coredata.dc:title: Efficient luminescent solar concentrators based on solvent polarity induced multiple-colored carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Guiju
Li, Tao
Han, Rui
Zhang, Guohua
Wang, Qianqian
Wang, Chao
Zhao, Haiguang
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) are large scale sunlight collector and can be used for building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV). Achieving high-performance LSCs requires fluorophores with broad absorption, high quantum yield and a large Stokes shift. Nevertheless, conventional high-efficiency LSCs typically rely on heavy metal-based quantum dots as fluorophores. Herein, we demonstrated highly efficient LSCs by deeply understanding the polarity of boron-doped carbon nanodots (B-C-dots) in different solvents or polymers, such as polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) or polystyrene (PS). The hydrothermally synthesized B-C-dots and undoped C-dots exhibit solvent dependent optical properties. Specifically, the emission peak shows a redshift when the C-dots are dispersed in ethanol, hexane and toluene, and the absorption edge shows a redshift with the increase of solvent polarity. For instance, the B-C-dots in toluene exhibit an absorption range of 300–600 nm and an emission peak of 605 nm, while in ethanol the absorption range extends to 300–650 nm and the emission peak is located at 438 nm. Once B-C-dots were dispersed in polymer matrices, the emission peak also shows various due to the different refractive index of polymers. As a proof-of-concept, we fabricated the LSCs (10 × 10 × 0.5 cm3) by selecting different types of polymers. We are able to achieve the LSCs with different colors and the optimized LSC based on B-C-dots/PS exhibits an optical efficiency of 5.48 % under natural sunlight illumination (69 mW/cm2). Our results pave a simple way to tune the colors and performance of the LSCs to achieve low-cost, highly efficient BIPV applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solvent dependent emission
Luminescent solar concentrators
Optical efficiency

Polarity effect on optical properties of C-dots

R. Han acknowledges the funding support from Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2023QF106 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of the B-C-dots and C-dots. (c) and (d) TEM image, size distribution and HRTEM image of C-dots (c) and B-C-dots (d). (e) XPS spectra of C-dots and B-C-dots, and high-resolution XPS spectra of the B-C-dots corresponding to C 1s, O 1s, N 1s and B 1s.

(a) Photographs of B-C-dots suspensions in toluene, ethanol and n-hexane under natural light and UV illumination. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra, (c) Normalized PL spectra and (d) PL decay curves of the B-C-dots in toluene, ethanol and n-hexane solution.

The photographs of the B-C-dots based LSCs based on waveguides of PMMA (a), PS (b), PVP (c) and PVA (d) as polymer, under indoor natural light and UV light. (e) The normalized PL intensity of the B-C-dots in the four polymers.

(a) Working mechanism and schematic diagram of optical efficiency of the B-C-dots based LSC-solar cell (LSC-SC) under different optical paths. (b) Optical efficiency of the B-C-dots/PMMA and B-C-dots/PS based LSCs illuminated under different optical distances. (c) J–V curves of the B-C-dots/PMMA based LSC-SC and SC under natural light (68 mW/cm2) illumination. (d) J–V curves of the B-C-dots/PS based LSC-SC and SC under natural light (69 mW/cm2) illumination. Insets are the relevant photographs of the LSCs under AM 1.5G sunlight illumination.
Luminescent lifetime and QY of the B-C-dots in different solvents.
External optical efficiency for the LSCs based on C-dots.

head.ce:title: Efficient luminescent solar concentrators based on solvent polarity induced multiple-colored carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Material Engineering, Yantai University, Yantai 264005 PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) are large scale sunlight collector and can be used for building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV). Achieving high-performance LSCs requires fluorophores with broad absorption, high quantum yield and a large Stokes shift. Nevertheless, conventional high-efficiency LSCs typically rely on heavy metal-based quantum dots as fluorophores. Herein, we demonstrated highly efficient LSCs by deeply understanding the polarity of boron-doped carbon nanodots (B-C-dots) in different solvents or polymers, such as polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) or polystyrene (PS). The hydrothermally synthesized B-C-dots and undoped C-dots exhibit solvent dependent optical properties. Specifically, the emission peak shows a redshift when the C-dots are dispersed in ethanol, hexane and toluene, and the absorption edge shows a redshift with the increase of solvent polarity. For instance, the B-C-dots in toluene exhibit an absorption range of 300–600 nm and an emission peak of 605 nm, while in ethanol the absorption range extends to 300–650 nm and the emission peak is located at 438 nm. Once B-C-dots were dispersed in polymer matrices, the emission peak also shows various due to the different refractive index of polymers. As a proof-of-concept, we fabricated the LSCs (10 × 10 × 0.5 cm3) by selecting different types of polymers. We are able to achieve the LSCs with different colors and the optimized LSC based on B-C-dots/PS exhibits an optical efficiency of 5.48 % under natural sunlight illumination (69 mW/cm2). Our results pave a simple way to tune the colors and performance of the LSCs to achieve low-cost, highly efficient BIPV applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, an increasing number of researchers have focused on harnessing solar energy to mitigate energy consumption in buildings. The rapid development of photovoltaic (PV) technologies (e.g. solar cells), which enables the power generation from solar energy, has made it possible to integrate them into building energy systems [1–3]. However, traditional commercial solar cells are usually opaque, limiting their application primarily to the roofs or building walls, rather than directly installed on windows, which wastes a lot of effective sunlight. Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs), as a new type of potential solar technology, have attracted wide attention for low costing power generation when combined with PV cells [4,5].

The LSC is a simple large-scale sunlight collection and conversion device comprising a transparent optical waveguide slab and luminescent materials, where the luminescent materials are embedded within or on the surface of the optical waveguide slab. The operating principle involves luminescent materials that absorb sunlight and subsequently re-emit photons. These newly generated photons undergo total internal reflection within the waveguide slab, directing them toward the smaller edges of the LSC, where attached PV cells convert the concentrated light into electricity [6,7]. Compared to other PV technologies, LSCs can be designed to be transparent or semi-transparent, lightweight, and they can absorb sunlight from various angles without solar tracking devices. Depending on adjustable colors, shapes and sizes, it can be integrated into building windows, decorative facades, parking canopies, etc., without affecting their appearance and day lighting. Therefore, LSCs-PV technology provides a way to achieve building integrated photovoltaics (BIPV) [7–9]. In general, the key to achieving high performance of the LSCs is the optical properties of luminescent materials, including wide absorption, high photoluminescence quantum yield (QY), large Stokes shift and long-term stability, as well as the refractive index of the waveguide [5,7,10,11]. The wide absorption can increase the absorption range of sunlight, high QY and large Stokes shift means the fluorophores can re-emit more photons and reduce the reabsorption loss during photons transmission, and large refractive index can increase the total reflection angle, so as to increase the total reflected light, thereby improving the optical efficiency of the device.

To date, various types of luminescent materials including organic dyes [12], metal ligands [13] and semiconductor quantum dots [14] have been employed to fabricate efficient LSCs. However, the use of these luminescent materials is often limited by high cost or the presence of toxic heavy metal elements, which hinders their potential commercial applications. Carbon nanodots (C-dots), a new class of luminescent material, have been employed in the integration of LSCs since 2017 [15]. C-dots have excellent optical properties, such as tunable excitation and emission, and high QY, comparable to traditional inorganic quantum dots, and have great advantages in facile synthesis, low cost, abundant functional groups and high stability [16–18]. Moreover, the C-dots is primarily composed of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and hydrogen, which is a truly green and eco-friendly luminescent nanomaterials. Through doping neighboring elements, the absorption and emission spectra of the C-dots can be effectively regulated, and the surface functional groups can be enriched. Thus, the synthesis of C-dots with high QY, large Stokes shift and luminescence stability for the construction of LSCs is conducive to improving the photoelectric conversion efficiency of the LSC-PV cell devices.

Besides the fluorescent materials, the selection of optical waveguide materials also affects the performance of LSCs, particularly due to the impact of refractive index factor. The waveguide materials in LSCs are usually made of transparent glass or polymer materials, such as polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) [18], polylauryl methacrylate (PLMA), polydimethicone (PDMS) [19], polyethylpyrrolidone (PVP) [20], polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [21], and polystyrene (PS) [22], etc. Although many reports focus on these waveguide materials, the systematical study of refractive index of waveguide materials on LSCs performance has not been widely reported. The refractive index determines how light propagates through the material, and the difference in refractive index between adjacent layers can leads to reflection, scattering, and losses of light. Therefore, it is essential to select a waveguide material with an appropriate refractive index to minimize these losses and optimize the light collection efficiency of LSCs.

In this work, we presented an innovative study of refractive index on LSCs performance by preparing the LSCs using boron doping C-dots (B-C-dots) embedded in various type of polymer waveguides. The C-dots was synthesized by nitration of pyrene and solvothermal method. The C-dots exhibits a solution-dependent emission spectra, which shows different luminescent colors in different solvents. By doping boron element, the C-dots in toluene shows an increased Stokes shift and a long lifetime. As a proof of concept, the LSCs were prepared by embedding the as-prepared B-C-dots in PMMA, PS, PVP and PVA polymers on glass substrates. Due to the difference of the solvents in these polymer solutions, the LSCs shows various emitted colors. In order to avoid the influence of solvent, we measured the PV properties of the LSC-PV system prepared by PMMA and PS as waveguide, and calculated the optical efficiency of these LSCs to study the influence of refractive index of waveguide materials on LSCs performance.

The synthesis of C-dots, both with and without boron doping, were carried out using a two-step method, as reported in previous literature [18]. First step, the trinitropyrene (TNP) precursor was chemically modified by the nitration of pyrene in concentrated HNO3. Second step, the B-C-dots were produced using a solvothermal method by mixing the TNP with boric acid in dimethylformamide (DMF). Subsequently, the B-C-dots were collected by centrifugation. The detailed preparation steps are shown in Supporting Information. For comparison, the C-dots without the addition of boric acid were synthesized using the same method.

The LSCs were fabricated by a drop-casting approach. We selected PMMA, PS, PVP and PVA as polymer waveguides for the fabrication of LSCs, respectively. Various polymers are dissolved in different solvents, including PMMA and PS dissolved in toluene, PVP dissolved in methanol, and PVA dissolved in deionized water. The detailed methods are shown in Supporting Information. Then ∼10 mg B-C-dots were added to each of the four polymer solutions and sonicated for 1 h to obtain clear, transparent and uniform dispersions. Lastly, the mixtures were drop-cast on the surface of glass substrates (10 × 10 × 0.5 cm3) and dried at room temperature overnight until all the solvent was evaporated.

The crystal structure, morphology and chemical structure of these C-dots were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The optical properties of these C-dots in solution or polymer matrices were characterized by absorption spectra, photoluminescence (PL) spectra, PLQY and fluorescence decay curves.

The PV properties of LSCs-PV systems and silicon PV cells were tested under natural sunlight illumination using Keithley 2400 Source Meter. The influence of optical propagation distance on device performance was tested under simulated sunlight illumination (40 mW/cm2) using a solar simulator (Newport). Details are included in Supporting Information. The external optical efficiency (ηopt) is defined as the ratio of the photons emitted from the edges of the LSCs to photons incident on the top surface, which can be calculated using the following equation [22]:
(1)
η
opt
=
P
out
×
A
edge
P
in
×
A
surface
=
P
out
P
in
× G
Where Pin and Pout are the power intensities of the LSC on the top surface and the edge, respectively. Aedge and Asurface are the areas of the top surface and the edges, respectively. G is the geometric factor, defined as a ratio of the top surface area relative to four edges of an LSC (G = Asurface/Aedges). In our case, the LSCs is coupled with a PV cell at the edge, the ηopt can also be simply calculated as [23]:
(2)
η
opt
=
J
LSCs
J
SC
× G
where J LSCs and J SC are the short circuit current density from the PV cell coupled with the LSCs and from the same PV cell under direct sunlight illumination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structure of the C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The B-C-dots and C-dots were synthesized through the nitration of pyrene and followed by the solvothermal method. To investigate the structure of these nanodots, the XRD characterization was conducted. As shown in Fig. 1 a, a broad diffraction peak located near 26.5° can be clearly seen, which is attributed to the amorphous carbon. In addition, a small sharp peak at 26.5° for the C-dots and a small peak at 44.5° for B-C-dots can be observed, which can be attributed to the (0 0 2) crystal plane and (1 0 1) crystal plane of the graphite carbon, respectively. This result indicates that both the B-C-dots and C-dots possess a certain crystalline structure in addition to amorphous phase. The chemical structure of the two kinds of C-dots was further investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1b, the two peaks appearing at approximately 2960 cm−1 and 2847 cm−1 are assigned to CH stretching vibrations [18,24]. The small peak at 1734 cm−1 corresponds to CO stretching vibrations [24–26]. The strong peaks at 1590 cm−1 and 1523 cm−1, along with the small peaks at 1454 cm−1 and 1411 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of the aromatic ring skeleton (CC) [24,27]. The peaks at 1335 cm−1 and 1192 cm−1 can be attributed to the CO stretching [18,28]. The absorption peaks at 1069 cm−1 and 1033 cm−1 can be assigned to CN stretching vibrations [18]. For the FT-IR spectra of C-dots and B-C-dots, the other various peaks at 896, 845, 796, 736 and 703 cm−1 are associated with the CH vinyl bonds or CH aromatic bonds [29]. Differently from that of C-dots, the FTIR spectrum of B-C-dots exhibits characteristic peaks of BC and BOH stretching vibrations at 1129 cm−1 and 1288 cm−1, respectively [29–31], which means that the carbon atoms of graphene structure are successfully substituted by boron atoms.

To further investigate the morphologies and microstructures of the B-C-dots and C-dots, the TEM and HRTEM characterizations were carried out, as shown in Fig. 1c,d. From Fig. 1c and d, it can be seen that both C-dots exhibit a spherical shape and are well-dispersed. For the C-dots, the size distribution ranges from 1.3 nm to 3.0 nm, with an average size of 2.11 nm (Fig. 1c). Similarly, the B-C-dots have a size distribution of 1.40–3.0 nm, with an average size of 2.14 nm (Fig. 1d). The insets in right-bottom in Fig. 1c and d display the HRTEM images of a typical individual C-dot and B-C-dot, respectively. Based on the HRTEM images of the two samples, it can be found that the lattice spacings of 2.17 Å for C-dots and 2.15 Å for B-C-dots are all correspond to the (100) plane of graphite carbon, indicating that the synthesized C-dots have a graphite-like structure, which is consistent with the XRD results.

Furthermore, XPS spectra were utilized to investigate the composition and possible bonding configurations of the two kinds of C-dots. Fig. 1e presents the XPS full spectra of the both C-dots and the elemental high-resolution XPS spectra of B-C-dots, which shows three distinct characteristic signals of C1s, O 1s and N 1s at about 284.8 eV, 532 eV and 406.6 eV, respectively. Notably, the XPS spectrum of the B-C-dots shows an additional weak signal of B 1s at about 186 eV, which proves the presence of B element in B-C-dots compared to C-dots. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s can be fitted into three peaks located at about 284.9 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.6 eV, which are attributed to CC, COC and OCO, respectively [32,33]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s was split into two peaks at 532.6 eV and 533.6 eV, which are ascribed to CO and CO, respectively [33]. The N 1s spectrum was split into two peaks at 399.4 eV and 401.1 eV, assigned to pyrrolic nitrogen and pyridinic nitrogen, respectively [26,34]. The high-resolution spectrum of B 1s shows the peak at 191.5 eV, corresponding to the BC bond [29]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C-dots are shown in Fig. S1. Compared to BC-dots, the significant difference is the absence of B 1s peak. Besides, in the O 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum, the CO content appears to be decreased, while the CO emerges to be increased. Similarly, the C 1s spectrum shows a decrease of CC and an increase of COC and OCO. The difference may be one of the factors contributing to different optical properties of the two C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polarity effect on optical properties of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The optical properties of these as-prepared B-C-dots and C-dots are shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. S2. Fig. 2a and Fig. S2a are the photographs of B-C-dots and C-dots suspensions in toluene, ethanol and n-hexane under natural light and UV illumination. It can be seen that the optical properties of C-dots and B-C-dots are strongly influenced by the solvents. For example, these nanodots exhibit different fluorescent colors when dispersed in toluene, ethanol, and n-hexane. It can be clearly seen that the B-C-dots emits red, blue and yellow-green fluorescence in toluene, ethanol and n-hexane, respectively under UV illumination (Fig. 2a). Similarly, the C-dots also show similar luminescence characteristics in corresponding solvent but with slight shifts in emission wavelengths (Fig. S2). Fig. 2b shows the UV–vis absorption spectra excited under 365 nm of B-C-dots in the above three solvents. It can be seen that the B-C-dots mainly shows two excitonic absorption band peaks, and the absorbance spectra at 300–400 nm is almost identical for the B-C-dots in various solvents. While the absorption edge is different, which is shifted from 570 nm in n-hexane to 600 nm in toluene and further to 650 nm for the B-C-dots in ethanol. Comparable changes also occurred in the absorption spectra of the C-dots in these solvents (Fig. S2b). It can be seen that the two excitonic absorption band peaks are located at the ranges of 385–415 nm and 450–500 nm, and the peaks position is different for the C-dots in various solvents. The absorption edge also shows a redshift with the C-dots dispersed in n-hexane, toluene and ethanol. It is well established that the n-hexane is a nonpolar solvent (relative polarity is 0.06), toluene is an ultralow polar solvent (relative polarity is 2.4), and in some cases can be approximated as a nonpolar solvent, whereas ethanol is a polar solvent (relative polarity is 4.3). According to the polarity of the three solvents and the absorption edges of the B-dots in these solvents, we can deduce the relationship between the polarity of the solvent and the absorption band edge. It is a non-linear relationship, which can be expressed as:
(3)
A e
=
A 1
+
A 2
e
p / t
where the Ae is the absorption edge and the p is the polarity of the solvent. A1, A2 and t are the estimated parameters. For the B-C-dots, A1, A2 and t are 545.1, 24.4 and 2.9, respectively. For the C-dots, A1, A2 and t are 356.5, 192.4 and 10.2, respectively. Thus, as the polarity of solvent increases, the absorption band edges undergo a redshift.

Fig. 2c shows the PL emission spectra of B-C-dots suspensions in toluene, ethanol and n-hexane, confirming that the emission wavelength of B-C-dots varies with the solvents. The B-C-dots in ethanol displays an emission peak at 438 nm, which redshifts to 570 nm in n-hexane and further to 605 nm in toluene. This shift in emission wavelength is consistent with the color changes observed under UV light in Fig. 2a. Similarly, when the C-dots is dispersed in the three solvents, the PL spectra also show a redshift, but the peak position changes slightly. The PL peaks of the C-dots in ethanol, n-hexane and toluene are located at 480 nm, 550 nm and 598 nm, respectively (Fig. S2c). These results reveal that the PL response of the B-C-dots and C-dots synthesized by pyrene nitration and solvothermal method is solvent-dependent, and through doping the B ions can adjust the PL wavelength of the nanodots in different solvents. The refractive index (n) of ethanol, n-hexane and toluene is 1.36, 1.39, and 1.49, respectively. As the n of solvent increases, the emission peak undergoes a redshift. Considering solvent solubility, ethanol is a water-soluble solvent, n-hexane and toluene are inwater-soluble solvents. Different solubility may affect the dispersion extent of the nanoparticles. In the water-soluble solvent, smaller C-dots are more easily dispersed, so the PL emission blueshifts due to the smaller particle size. In the inwater-soluble solvents, the solvent–solute interaction can initiate the self-assembly so as to increase the size of the C-dots [17]. Therefore, compared with the C-dots in water-soluble solvent, the PL emission of the C-dots in inwater-soluble solvents show a redshift. Meanwhile, for the C-dots in inwater-soluble solvents, with the increase of solvent polarity, the influence on the electron distribution of the nanodots increases, and the excited state can be better stabilized, making the electrons easier to transfer to a lower energy level, resulting in the PL emission redshift. This solvent-dependent behavior is very similar to the “solvatochromism” commonly observed in the organic dyes [35,36], suggesting that this B-C-dots can be used as fluorescent probes or color indicators for measuring solvents.

Based on the absorption and emission spectra, the Stokes shift can be calculated (Fig. S3). It can be seen that the B-C-dots in n-hexane shows the first excitonic absorption peak at 444 nm, the emission peak centered at 570 nm, and the Stokes shift is 0.62 eV (Fig. S3a). For the B-C-dots in toluene, the first excitonic absorption peak centered at 463 nm and the emission peak centered at 605 nm with the Stokes shift of 0.63 eV (Fig. S3b). Whereas, for the B-C-dots in ethanol, the first excitonic absorption peak centered at 469 nm, and emission peak centered at 438 nm, which shows an anti-Stokes shift of 0.19 eV (Fig. S3c), which will lead to large reabsorption energy loss in the use of LSCs. The Stokes shift of the B-C-dots in n-hexane is very close to that in toluene, but the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the PL peaks for the B-C-dots in n-hexane (146 nm) is larger than that in toluene (88 nm). Therefore, in the application of LSCs, the reabsorption loss for the B-C-dots in n-hexane is larger.

Fig. 2d is the PL decay curves of the B-C-dots in the three solvents, which show dual-exponential decay. The fitting data presented in Table 1 reveal that the average lifetime of the B-C-dots decreases from 7.21 ns in ethanol to 5.48 ns in n-hexane and further to 2.09 ns in toluene. This trend aligns with the change in PL spectra, indicating that the PL lifetime gradually decreases as the PL emission spectrum redshifts. The QY measurements of the B-C-dots were tested using an integrating sphere coupled with a PL spectrometer (λex = 380 nm). By comparing the spectra of the B-C-dots and corresponding blank solvent (Fig. S4), the absolute QY of the B-C-dots in n-hexane and toluene are 76.38 % and 86.56 %, respectively. For the B-C-dots in ethanol, the excitation and emission spectra can not be well divided, the QY can not be calculated very accurately. Based on the QY and PL lifetime, we can also calculate the radiative (kr) and nonradiative (knr) decay rates of the B-C-dots in different solvents. The QY can be calculated using the follow equation:
(4)
Q Y =
k r
k r
+
k
n r
The PL lifetime can be calculated using the equation:
(5)
τ =
1
k r
+
k
n r
Using the Eqs. (4) and (5), the calculated kr and knr of the B-C-dots in toluene were 4.14 × 10−1 ns−1 and 6.43 × 10−2 ns−1, respectively. For the B-C-dots in n-hexane, the kr and knr were calculated to be 1.40 × 10−1 ns−1 and 4.31 × 10−2 ns−1, respectively. The results indicate that the B-C-dots show a dominate radiative recombination mechanism. In addition, we measured the PL spectra of the B-C-dots in exposure to a UV light (365 nm light emitting device with an intensity of 30 mW/cm2, equal to 6 sun illumination) after longtime illumination to evaluate the photo-stability of the B-C-dots. As shown in Fig. S5, after 10 h illumination, the integrated PL intensity of B-C-dots in toluene maintains over 84 % of its initial intensity. While for the B-C-dots in hexane, the integrated PL intensity retains 68 % of its initial value. This means that in toluene, the B-C-dots is more stable.

The above results mean that optical properties of the C-dots, including the absorption spectra, PL emission and lifetime, are all influenced by the solvent. Considering the different solubility, polarity and n of the three solvents, the properties of the solvent molecules and the surface structure of the C-dots may play a critical role in the optical properties of C-dots suspended in various solvents. The interaction between the solvent and the nanodot surface may lead to changes in the energy levels and electronic transitions, thereby affecting the optical properties. For example, when the C-dots were dispersed in water-soluble solvents, the absorption band edge was redshifted, and the PL emission peak showed a blueshift, indicating an anti-Stokes phenomenon. For the C-dots in inwater-soluble solvents (n-hexane and toluene), with the increase of solvent polarity, both the absorption and PL spectra were redshifted, and the lifetime was decreased. This solvent-dependent behavior offers opportunities for tuning the optical properties of C-dots through solvent selection, which could be beneficial for various applications such as sensor development, optoelectronic devices, and color indicators.

The above emission color change pertains to the C-dots dispersed in different solvents. In the practical applications, the nanodots usually dispersed in solids. To investigate the optical performance of the C-dots in solids, we employed the B-C-dots as emitters, PMMA, PS, PVP and PVA as waveguide to fabricate the LSCs. Fig. 3 a–d show the photos of B-C-dots/PMMA, B-C-dots/PS, B-C-dots/PVP and B-C-dots/PVA based LSCs under indoor natural light and UV light illumination. It can be seen that the words positioned at the bottom of the LSCs are distinctly visible under indoor natural light, meanwhile bright fluorescence emits from the edges under UV light. However, the emitted light's color varies depending on the polymer used. To quantify the color change of the nanodots in different polymer films, we measured the PL emission spectra of the four samples, as shown in Fig. 3e. It can be seen that the emission peaks of B-C-dots in PMMA and PS polymer films are located at 604 nm and 596 nm, respectively. While for the B-C-dots in PVP and PVA polymer films, the PL spectra show two emission peaks, and the main peaks are located at 654 nm and 611 nm, respectively. For the B-C-dots/PMMA and B-C-dots/PS films, the polymer solvent is toluene. The difference of emission peak is due to the different polymers. While for the B-C-dots/PVP and B-C-dots/PVA films, the polymer solvents are methanol and water, respectively. The difference of the emission peaks may be attribute to the interaction between the solvent and polymer. The above results show that similar to the nanodots in solvents, the emission spectra of our B-C-dots in polymer films are affected by the types of polymers and solvents, and the solvent may exert a more pronounced influence on the luminescence performance.

In summary, we successfully demonstrate efficient LSCs with multiple colors by using solvent-polarity determined B-C-dots. C-dots and B-C-dots were hydrothermally synthesized by the nitration of pyrene without or with boric acid. Both types of C-dots exhibit solvent dependent optical properties, including the redshift of absorption edges as solvent polarity increases, as well as the redshift of emission peaks and a decrease of PL lifetimes when the C-dots are dispersed in ethanol, hexane and toluene. Additionally, when the C-dots embedded in polymer matrices, the emission peak varies due to the differences in both the polymer solvents and polymer types. As a demonstration of their practical application, we achieved highly efficient LSCs by dispersing B-C-dots in PMMA or PS polymers. The large-scale LSCs (10 × 10 × 0.5 cm3) based on the B-C-dots/PMMA exhibit an optical efficiency of 4.71 % under natural sunlight illumination (68 mW/cm2). When the waveguide material is replaced by PS, which has a higher refractive index, the optical efficiency increased to 5.48 % under natural sunlight illumination (69 mW/cm2). These results demonstrate a straightforward approach to adjusting the colors and performance of the LSCs for cost-effective and highly efficient BIPV applications. Furthermore, these findings hold significant potential for the application of these nanodots in sensors, color indicators, and other optoelectronic devices, broadening their utility across various technological domains.

Guiju Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Tao Li: Data curation. Rui Han: Investigation, Formal analysis. Guohua Zhang: Data curation. Qianqian Wang: Data curation. Chao Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Haiguang Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: G.J. Liu acknowledges the funding support from Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2022QE188), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12304471) and Youth Innovation Team Program of Colleges and Universities of Shandong Province (2023KJ237). R. Han acknowledges the funding support from Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2023QF106).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2025.01.196.
